critic_raw:
The experimental design is thoughtful and addresses many common failure modes explicitly. Temporal splitting, confound auditing, and stratified evaluation are all present. However, several substantive methodological issues remain.

**Issue 1: The baseline is not a fair comparison — the text-only transformer is fine-tuned, while text embeddings in the primary model are frozen.**

The design states the primary model uses 'pre-trained transformer (e.g., DistilBERT) applied to email body and subject; no task-specific fine-tuning to isolate semantic signal.' The baseline, however, is 'a pre-trained transformer fine-tuned on text only.' This is not a controlled comparison of modalities — it is a comparison of frozen vs. fine-tuned text representations, confounded with the presence/absence of metadata. Any performance difference between models is attributable to one of two sources: (a) metadata helping, or (b) fine-tuning helping. The design cannot distinguish these. The claim 'Does metadata improve text-only performance?' cannot be answered by this comparison.

For the comparison to be valid, either: (a) both models should use frozen embeddings (isolating metadata contribution purely), or (b) both should use fine-tuned embeddings (testing metadata as an addition to a fully-optimized text model). An additional ablation — fine-tuned text + metadata — would resolve this. Without it, a positive result for the primary model is uninterpretable with respect to the stated hypothesis.

Evidence test: Run the primary model with fine-tuned text embeddings (not frozen) and compare against frozen-embedding + metadata. If the gap narrows or reverses, fine-tuning was the driver.

**Issue 2: Precision at 95% recall threshold is tuned on validation; the specific threshold tuning creates a subtle circularity with the primary metric.**

The primary metric is 'precision at 95% recall on the test set.' Hyperparameters are tuned on validation to maximize this metric. The threshold that achieves 95% recall on validation will not, in general, achieve 95% recall on the test set — class distributions and score distributions shift across time windows. If the reported test metric is 'evaluate precision at the threshold that achieves 95% recall on test,' this is fine but slightly underspecified. If the threshold is fixed from validation and then precision is reported on test at that same threshold, the reported recall on test may not be 95%, making the metric meaningless as stated. The design does not specify this procedure clearly.

Evidence test: Report actual recall achieved on the test set at the validation-derived threshold alongside precision. If recall deviates from 95%, the primary metric comparison is invalid.

**Issue 3: Sender reputation features at arrival time may embed post-delivery information for the training period.**

The design states: 'Derived from reputation feeds at arrival; not updated post-delivery.' However, reputation feeds are typically updated continuously by third-party vendors based on global reports, sandbox verdicts, and abuse signals. During training data collection (Jan–Sep 2022), emails that were malicious would have been reported to reputation services. When reputation signals are queried for those emails — even 'at arrival time' in the log record — if the reputation feed value is stored at logging time and those logs are used for training, the question is when exactly the feed value was recorded. If reputation feed values were cached at the time of analyst verdict (30+ days post-arrival) rather than strictly at message arrival, reputation scores for known-malicious emails in the training set would reflect post-delivery abuse reports — a form of label leakage through reputation features. This is distinct from the 'reputation leakage audit' described, which checks for internal signals, not external feed timing.

Evidence test: Audit timestamp of reputation feed lookups vs. email arrival timestamps in the log schema. For a sample of confirmed-malicious training emails, compare reputation score at arrival vs. 30 days later. If scores change substantially, leakage is present.

**Issue 4: The 'novel domain' stratification conflates sender novelty with attack novelty, potentially masking the most important failure mode for spear-phishing.**

Spear-phishing often uses lookalike domains or hijacked legitimate domains. A domain 'novel in the test window' may be genuinely new infrastructure or may be a typosquat of a high-reputation training domain. The stratification as designed will group these together. The model's failure mode on hijacked-legitimate-domain attacks (where reputation signals are actively misleading — high reputation, legitimate age, valid DKIM) is the most operationally critical case and will not be surfaced by the novel-domain stratification. The design's confound control addresses domain memorization but not the case where reputation signals actively contradict the ground truth.

Evidence test: Create a separate stratum: test emails where all reputation signals are 'clean' (DKIM pass, domain age > 1 year, no blacklist flags) but ground truth is malicious. Report precision at 95% recall for this stratum. If performance collapses, the metadata features provide false confidence on exactly the attacks they are meant to help with.

**Issue 5: AUC-PR as a secondary metric is redundant with and less informative than the primary metric in this setting, but more importantly, bootstrap CIs stratified by month are correct only if months are approximately i.i.d., which temporal drift violates.**

The confidence intervals use bootstrap stratified by month. Bootstrap validity requires exchangeability of the resampled units. Under temporal drift, later months have systematically different attack patterns than earlier months — months are not exchangeable. Bootstrapping across months will produce confidence intervals that understate uncertainty at the edges of the time window and overstate uncertainty in the aggregate. The permutation test for model comparison inherits the same assumption. This doesn't invalidate the point estimate but means the reported CIs and p-values may be misleading.

Evidence test: Compare bootstrap CIs with a block bootstrap (treating each month as a block, resampling months with replacement). If CIs widen substantially, temporal correlation is material and the standard bootstrap is anti-conservative.
all_issues_raised: The frozen-embedding primary model vs. fine-tuned baseline confounds modality contribution with representation quality, making the core hypothesis untestable.
The precision-at-95%-recall primary metric is underspecified with respect to whether the threshold is fixed from validation or re-derived on test, creating potential metric invalidity.
Reputation feed values may reflect post-delivery abuse reports rather than true arrival-time scores, introducing label leakage through the metadata features.
The novel-domain stratification misses the operationally critical failure mode where reputation signals are actively clean for spear-phishing attacks (hijacked or aged domains).
Bootstrap CIs stratified by month assume month exchangeability, which temporal drift violates, potentially producing anti-conservative confidence intervals and p-values.
